title: Low-Current Tracing
learningObjectives: Trace circuits while keeping current in the micro-to-milliamp range
Balance voltage and very high resistance
Ensure circuit validity while currents are tiny
introText: Troubleshooting sensitive circuits means working with tiny currents. You'll set high resistance and moderate voltage to keep current extremely low while the loop stays valid.

steps.instructionText: Wire a DC source and a resistor into a closed loop.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Loop is valid. Ready for low-current work.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Connect both terminals of the source and resistor to close the circuit.

steps.instructionText: Raise the resistor until current drops below 0.002 A.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Current is under 2 mA — excellent for careful tracing.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Increase resistance or reduce voltage until current is below 0.002 A.

steps.instructionText: Keep the source near 6–9V while maintaining the low current limit.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Voltage remains usable (≥6V) while current stays minimal.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Raise the source slightly while keeping resistance high so current stays low.

questions.questionText: Why keep current tiny while probing sensitive circuits?
options: To avoid damaging parts and altering measurements
questions.explanation: Low current protects components and prevents disturbing the circuit under test.
High resistance limits current even when voltage is moderate.
